subject: CS-CV
description: The project is initiated with the goal of improving few-shot visual classification. The core idea is to investigate whether self-supervised pre-training on in-domain, unlabeled data can produce more robust feature representations compared to standard supervised pre-training on large, out-of-domain datasets like ImageNet.
reason: This is the first step of the research project, establishing a clear direction. It formalizes the initial, high-level idea (the required 'research_goals') into a concrete set of objectives and creates an initial 'research_plan' to guide the subsequent work.

subject: CS-CV
description: A detailed methodological scheme is designed. We select MoCo v2 as the self-supervised learning algorithm, a ResNet-50 backbone, and Prototypical Networks as the downstream few-shot classifier. The miniImageNet dataset is chosen for the pilot study, where a subset of classes will be used for unlabeled pre-training and the remaining for the few-shot evaluation task.
reason: Following the initial proposal (e_01), this event translates the high-level research goals into a concrete experimental design. It's a necessary step to define the specific algorithms, architectures, and datasets needed before any implementation or experimentation can begin, creating the initial 'method_scheme'.

subject: CS-CV
description: A small-scale pilot experiment is conducted to validate the designed scheme. We implement the MoCo v2 pre-training on a subset of miniImageNet classes and then evaluate the learned features on a 5-way 1-shot classification task using Prototypical Networks. The primary goals are to debug the implementation and get an initial performance signal.
reason: With the methodological approach defined in 'method_scheme' (e_02), the logical next step is to perform a pilot study. This allows for a low-cost sanity check of the proposed method and the implementation codebase before committing to resource-intensive main experiments, generating the first set of 'experiment_results'.

subject: CS-CV
description: The results from the pilot study are analyzed. The self-supervised pre-trained model shows a significant accuracy improvement (+8%) over a randomly initialized baseline but still underperforms the standard ImageNet pre-trained baseline by 5%. This analysis concludes that the core idea is sound, but the pre-training needs to be more extensive or refined to close the performance gap.
reason: After obtaining initial 'experiment_results' from the pilot study (e_03), a formal 'analysis' is necessary to interpret the findings in the context of the original 'research_goals'. This step determines whether the project is on the right track and informs the decision for subsequent, larger-scale experiments, leading to an updated 'research_plan'.

subject: CS-CV
description: Based on the promising pilot results, the main experiments are launched. The MoCo v2 pre-training is scaled up to use all available unlabeled data from the miniImageNet training split for a larger number of epochs. The resulting feature extractor is then rigorously benchmarked on standard 5-way 1-shot and 5-way 5-shot tasks.
reason: The analysis of the pilot study (e_04) validated the core idea and updated the 'research_plan' to proceed with larger-scale experiments. This event executes that plan, using the established 'method_scheme' to generate a comprehensive set of 'experiment_results' needed to properly evaluate the hypothesis.

subject: CS-CV
description: A detailed analysis of the main experiment results is performed. The scaled-up self-supervised pre-training now outperforms the ImageNet-supervised baseline by 3% on the 5-way 1-shot task and 2.5% on the 5-way 5-shot task. This confirms the project's central hypothesis that in-domain self-supervision can be superior for few-shot learning.
reason: Following the completion of the main experiments (e_05), a thorough 'analysis' is required to interpret the new 'experiment_results' and evaluate them against the original 'research_goals'. The positive outcome justifies starting the writing process by creating the first 'paper_paragraph' for the results section and updating the 'research_plan' to focus on manuscript preparation and supplementary analyses.

subject: CS-CV
description: With the successful main results confirmed, a significant portion of the paper's 'Methodology' section is drafted. This draft describes the self-supervised pre-training stage using MoCo v2 and the downstream few-shot classification setup with Prototypical Networks, based on the final experimental design.
reason: Following the successful analysis of main experiments (e_06), the 'research_plan' was updated to focus on manuscript preparation. This 'writing' event is the natural execution of that plan, translating the technical 'method_scheme' into a descriptive 'paper_paragraph' for the methods section of the research paper.

subject: CS-CV
description: To test the generalizability of the findings, the team runs the same experimental setup on a different benchmark: the CUB-200-2011 fine-grained classification dataset. This involves performing in-domain self-supervised pre-training and evaluating on few-shot tasks created from CUB. The goal is to see if the performance gains over the ImageNet baseline hold in a different data domain.
reason: After confirming the main hypothesis on miniImageNet (e_06), a crucial next step is to verify the method's generalizability. Running the protocol on a different benchmark (CUB-200-2011), as part of the supplementary analysis mentioned in the 'research_plan', provides stronger evidence and requires applying the existing 'method_scheme' to produce new 'experiment_results'.

subject: CS-CV
description: The results from the CUB-200-2011 experiments are analyzed. The in-domain self-supervised pre-training again outperforms the ImageNet baseline, this time by an even larger margin (4.5% on 5-way 1-shot) due to the fine-grained nature of the dataset. This result strongly supports the generalizability of the proposed method.
reason: After running generalization experiments on a new dataset (e_08), a dedicated 'analysis' is the necessary next step to interpret the new 'experiment_results'. This analysis confirms whether the method's effectiveness holds in a different domain, which supports the overall 'research_goals' and provides key findings to be documented in a 'paper_paragraph'.

subject: CS-CV
description: With the main hypothesis validated on two datasets, the team drafts the 'Introduction' and 'Conclusion' sections of the paper. The introduction frames the problem, presents the core hypothesis, and summarizes the key findings from both miniImageNet and CUB experiments, while the conclusion discusses the implications and potential future work.
reason: After successfully analyzing the main (e_06) and generalization (e_09) experiments, the project has a complete set of strong results. Drafting the framing sections of the paper (introduction and conclusion) is a logical next step to synthesize the 'research_goals' and existing result summaries ('paper_paragraph') into a coherent narrative for the final manuscript.

subject: CS-CV
description: To further strengthen the paper, an ablation study is designed and executed. We investigate the impact of the amount of unlabeled data used for pre-training on miniImageNet, evaluating performance when using 25%, 50%, and 75% of the data compared to the full 100% used in the main experiment.
reason: With the main hypothesis confirmed and the paper's narrative structure drafted (e_10), a logical next step is to conduct ablation studies to provide deeper insights and preempt potential reviewer questions. This experiment uses the established 'method_scheme' to investigate a key hyperparameter (amount of pre-training data), generating new 'experiment_results' that will add significant value to the final paper.

subject: CS-CV
description: The results of the ablation study are analyzed, revealing a strong positive correlation between the amount of unlabeled pre-training data and final few-shot accuracy. Performance degrades gracefully as the data is reduced from 100% to 25%, but even the 25% model significantly outperforms the randomly initialized baseline, highlighting the data-efficiency of the approach.
reason: After generating new 'experiment_results' from the ablation study (e_11), an 'analysis' is necessary to interpret these findings. This step provides crucial insights into the method's sensitivity to the amount of pre-training data, strengthening the paper's claims and helping to finalize the 'research_plan' for manuscript completion.

subject: CS-CV
description: The team writes the full 'Experiments' section of the paper, integrating the main results on miniImageNet, the generalization results on CUB, and the findings from the ablation study. This involves structuring the section, creating tables to summarize the quantitative results, and providing clear descriptions of each experimental setup and its outcome.
reason: With all major experiments and analyses completed (e_06, e_09, e_12), and several paper sections drafted (e_07, e_10), the 'research_plan' now calls for manuscript completion. This 'writing' event is the critical step of consolidating the various 'paper_paragraph' snippets and experimental findings into a complete and structured 'Experiments' section, moving closer to a full paper draft.

subject: CS-CV
description: While reviewing the drafted paper, the team identifies a potential counter-argument: perhaps the gains are simply from continued training, not from scratch training on in-domain data. To address this, the experimental scheme is revised to include a new baseline: fine-tuning the ImageNet-pretrained model on the in-domain unlabeled data using the MoCo v2 objective before few-shot evaluation.
reason: As the paper draft (`paper_paragraph`) is being consolidated, a potential weakness in the experimental comparison is identified. This `scheme_design` event represents a realistic pivot to strengthen the paper's argument by updating the `method_scheme` and `research_plan` to include a more rigorous baseline, proactively addressing a likely reviewer concern before submission.

subject: CS-CV
description: The team executes the newly designed experiment. The ImageNet-pretrained ResNet-50 model is fine-tuned on miniImageNet's unlabeled training data using the MoCo v2 objective. The resulting model is then evaluated on the standard 5-way 1-shot and 5-shot benchmarks to create the new baseline result.
reason: Following the decision to add a stronger baseline in e_14, this `main_experiments` event is the direct execution of that updated `research_plan` and `method_scheme`. It is a necessary step to generate the comparative `experiment_results` required to address the potential counter-argument identified during the writing process.

subject: CS-CV
description: The results of the new fine-tuning baseline are analyzed. This new baseline (ImageNet pre-train + MoCo fine-tuning) performs better than the standard ImageNet baseline, but still underperforms our main method (in-domain MoCo from scratch) by a small margin (1.5%). This analysis confirms that training from scratch on in-domain data provides the most effective features, strongly reinforcing the paper's main claim.
reason: After running the new baseline experiment in e_15, a dedicated 'analysis' is essential to interpret these critical 'experiment_results'. This step directly addresses the counter-argument raised in e_14, and its positive outcome solidifies the paper's conclusion, allowing for the generation of a new 'paper_paragraph' with this finding and an update to the 'research_plan' to finalize the manuscript.

subject: CS-CV
description: The team revises the 'Experiments' section to include the results from the new, stronger baseline (ImageNet pre-train + MoCo fine-tuning). The 'Introduction' and 'Conclusion' are also updated to reflect how this final comparison solidifies the paper's central claim. A full draft of the paper is assembled by combining all written sections.
reason: Following the crucial analysis of the new baseline in e_16, the 'research_plan' is to finalize the manuscript. This 'writing' step is essential for integrating the latest 'paper_paragraph' findings into the main text, creating a complete and cohesive first draft of the entire paper, which is a major milestone before submission.

subject: CS-CV
description: The research project is concluded. All core research questions have been answered, supported by comprehensive experiments, and a full, revised manuscript has been drafted. The project is now considered complete and ready for submission to a peer-reviewed venue.
reason: The project has reached a natural conclusion. The initial hypothesis was validated on multiple datasets, supported by ablation studies and a strong new baseline, and all findings have been consolidated into a complete paper draft (e_17). No further research is needed to support the central claims, making this the logical endpoint of the research timeline.
